author: defiantwyvern
content:
@ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics I think the important thing is that producers now need to pass on some of those fees to customers. What polymer was worth previously is maybe not what it is "worth" now. But really polymer is only worth what people are willing to pay, which depends on what it can be used for.

Just like when stuff gets taxed more IRL, consumers usually end up eating some of that cost.

author: GoldenLover | BubblePop
content: I mean yes and no. While a single company may get only taxed a single time, a product may get taxed several times before its “final” state.  Take a house for example, the lumber is taxed when it’s sold to the builder. The builder then pays taxes to workers or whatever supplies are needed. Then when the house is complete and sold by a real estate agent, that is taxed as well.  Like any government, they seek to reach into every transaction.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
@Chri.z is in a challenging position. He's driving the economy towards stability, which is his number one priority. At the same time, we knew that asset emission rates via redemptions was quite high at the start. That was also by design, because we wanted to ensure that adjustments to the economy, while expressed in tightening, would still leave the early players feeling rewarded. This is opposed to the inverse in which we went very strict at launch and loosened up. That would have been the conservative approach, but would have rewarded players joining later. Which isn't fair to our early adopters. We're also a bit hamstrung as we're still pushing through feature development, so the econ loops present are really the toolset he has to work with. 

The result, you can place higher confidence on the economy tightening vs loosening, especially in the near term. So get in there and grind Labs as much as you can, because future players will be working under larger constraints and more challenging reward systems. I can tell you with certainty, real ship crafting in the future is going to be far more resource and time intensive than anything you're seeing right now.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: @[SAG] Swift42 anyone who has a 0.9% market fee is not going to risk getting banned by blatantly manipulating the market.  I also think you are underestimating the amount of effort it will take to have enough components/goods to manipulate the market.  Plus, you would also need enough liquidity on the buy side to sell all those goods in the week where crafting fees are elevated.  And tbh even if crafting fees doubled, people would probably either craft anyways or simply not build x.  Or they would go complain on discord and track down the price anomaly to the random person with the goods. I doubt these people would buy any exorbitant prices by the manipulator.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
Question:
I brought my brother to Sage some weeks ago. 
Some days ago I sent him my referral link, because I heard that he wants to buy a large ship.
He bought the ship, but nothing was shown in my dashboard. It turned out that he clicked on some third-party site on a market link before, without knowing that it contained a referral link.
And it seems that the first referral code is permanently attached to an account. So some sneaky mf got the money.
So essentially I brought him here, but the reward was given to someone else.
Question 1: How is this even allowed?
Question 2: Can he stop the address from getting any further rewards?

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: yeah the IMP Tap design was in response to the land mining ship request. Technically it was meant to attach to an asteroid as all the IMPs are meant for. but there appears to be competing sentiments around that. The IMP Tap output versus a land claim with a mining rig wouldn't even be in the same class. The IMP Tap was more for the solo miner who just wants to go find some unclaimed land and mine in solitude but not really meant for mega enterprise purposes.

author: Support Line | Star Atlas Mod [STAR]
content: cool, really appreciate the feedback. i'm fine with going with the crowd on that. but some added clarification, tip/tap would only be able to mine on unclaimed land or land they own. but if they own that land they would be much better off staking it and dropping some mining rigs rather than hand mining with a ship. so it leaves no good scaling solution for the tip/tap on land except for the fun of exploration.

content: I think that S.A. will attract more interest, in the form of money from people that have, money, more, to the extent that they stick to what they have published.  This, precisely because you can only commit capital, rationally, if you feel as if you can plan on an outcome in the future.  When things get changed back and forth, it tells you that you can not plan with much of a probabilistic outcome.

author: ! ᚺEIM | VɪᴋɪɴɢᴇɴBᴇɴɴʏViking
content: Mods and sandbox type of stuff is one thing, and I'm sure there will be an area for that type of stuff, but when it comes to official planets in the SA universe, I think it makes sense having it around that price, considering the prices of other high value assets like Titans for instance.  I think there will be many valuable activities and assets to work towards for the average Joe, without diluting the value of the assets in the "whale" price range.  Remember, it's going to take whales and many $1M assets for it to be profitable for the average Joe to operate an airbike, for instance. The $$ has to come from somewhere and there must be expensive, desirable things to work towards or there will be no motivation to grind if everyone gets an entire planet for $100

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: I strongly support the direction @SW4GN3R is taking on focusing on the building out the main components of the game and let the community developers build the tools. Some of the automation should/must be done by the SA team to make the game enjoyable. However the project will scale better if minor tools are provided by the eco-system.

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: The SLY tool is a great example of what a community developer can produce. Now of course an individual may not feel comfortable with using such tool. That is understandable.

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: That said some of the automation that are minimum requirement for a game like sage that gives it the standard RTS functionality is essential for the team to develop. Ability to set an asteroid system to mine with certain configuration and return back to home base for refueling is important functionality that should be part of Sage and not something developed by community developers. I hope such automation will become part of SAGE sooner rather than later. The team should not depend upon community to deliver such automation. Having a tool such as Aephia built using Flipside make sense but not fundamental automation for the gaming experience. My 2 Atlas.

content: We don't want the product undermined either. I think you just have to get comfortable with the fact that external tool innovation is going to be supported in the sense that we aren't censoring or prohibiting that innovation. The community itself is expected to be part of that vetting process, and we don't have capacity to analyze all of these tools on top of building core systems.

author: [AEP] Boen
content:
SA team, question re Polis locking and what happens in the event of switching a wallet:
What is the situation with Polis staking positions? If you have Polis locked for 5 years, and your wallet gets compromised, the Polis is stake locked (although the daily distributions would be at risk). But do you know if the team has discussed a function to migrate that Polis stake safely?

And in the event you want to move your hot wallet staked Polis to cold storage (whether or not a compromise has occurred), what is the process there? Is there a custodian process that safely migrates the hot wallet key so that the Polis can never be compromised due to a hot wallet key vs cold?

Thanks

content: That's great to hear. Given that most of us have staked for 5 years and even a compromised wallet likely wouldn't be able to lose those Polis funds, then I suspect we have some time for this dev to be created. author: Chri.z [STAR]
content:
I've explained this exhaustively a few times but I'll do it again. 
Person A has wood and person B has stones. 
the exchange rate is 1 wood for 1 stone
each wood and stone is also worth 1 gold = 1 USDC
So, 1 wood = 1 USDC = 1 gold = 1 stone
Now, the exchange rate of gold to USDC changes dramatically so now 1 gold = 2 USDC. 
What happens to prices? 
1 wood = 1 USDC = 0.5 gold = 1 stone. 
The only thing that happened is the value of gold changed. 
As the economic planner, if I start messing around with wood and stone, I've made a big mistake. There's nothing wrong with wood or stone. It's simply that gold has changed value

What bothered me (and talking to other guild members they had the same impression) was:   1 the music didn't "match" the mood presented 2 had a lot of “loose” information in the trailer, without specifying exactly anything to be shown.  Like, we know there's everything there, and what's not is coming soon. but a layman in Star Atlas would not be able to identify a focus or characteristic to be impacted and expand their knowledge about the game.  Once again I emphasize, what was presented and how it was shown with the soundtrack in Breakpoint was incredible, and perhaps I expected something like that.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: I've explained this exhaustively a few times but I'll do it again.  Person A has wood and person B has stones.  the exchange rate is 1 wood for 1 stone each wood and stone is also worth 1 gold = 1 USDC So, 1 wood = 1 USDC = 1 gold = 1 stone Now, the exchange rate of gold to USDC changes dramatically so now 1 gold = 2 USDC.  What happens to prices?  1 wood = 1 USDC = 0.5 gold = 1 stone.  The only thing that happened is the value of gold changed.  As the economic planner, if I start messing around with wood and stone, I've made a big mistake. There's nothing wrong with wood or stone. It's simply that gold has changed value

reply.preview: I've explained this exhaustively a few times but I'll do it again. Person A has wood and person B has stones. the exchange rate is 1 wood for 1 stone each wood and stone is also worth 1 gold = 1 USDC So, 1 wood = 1 USDC = 1 gold = 1 stone Now, the exchange rate of gold to USDC changes dramatically so now 1 gold = 2 USDC. What happens to prices? 1 wood = 1 USDC = 0.5 gold = 1 stone. The only thing that happened is the value of gold changed. As the economic planner, if I start messing around with wood and stone, I've made a big mistake. There's nothing wrong with wood or stone. It's simply that gold has changed value

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
If it was me I would have a separate content creator prize pool and I could be disqualified for all I care. #1 , #2 , #3 prizes for the content creators that creates the most buzz for big events like Golden Ticket. 
Every wallet that enters 100 tickets or more into the golden ticket draw gets to receive a CC (content creator) token, one possible CC token per wallet per draw. These tickets would be used to vote for the content creator that created the most golden ticket buzz.
Content creators might have to pay Atlas or golden tickets... to enter the draw, then their name/wallet would be put on a list for other players to vote on. This CC draw would be on the last week of golden tickets also.

From my perspective as a content creator, I like promoting the game, the lore, the progress, pictures, video. Why should I promote betting, even though it might help the team/game? Give the content creator a reason to want to promote the event with a good prize and then you could get more on board. A content creator challenge. Even a cool custom mug or hoodie would be cool but they cost USD. 
Just some thoughts.

content:
At what point of the game will we get more skills into the game??  currently its just clicking, and very very repetitive, which in the long run makes it boring and no fun.

Is there any plan to get more minigame style into the game so that miners need to aim their laser beams, scanners need to swipe their screen clean or crafters need to weld their own shit and gives different rarity/quantity on your preformance.

This are just stupid examples, but the idea is to get more diverse gameplay even if you are doing a repetative task.

In the same time this will hopefully combat bots as well, require / incentivise people to play instead of automate

author: 𝕘𝕣𝕚𝕤𝕥
content:
Everything changes with combat. Then the game becomes some cosmic hyper chess strategy game. 

Then the talk dissolves and the action happens. Some will find flow state in those infinite game loops. Some will sort of fade away—think the word is faded—and find their fun in the real time sweat action of a shooter or combat racer. Then we see the crucible for the OG guilds. Guilds have already set the table, dropping each guild in its place—well, where they imagine they will be.. .

 And that’s when guilds who have been doing this for a long time arrive. If the game is 40% of what it could be that will happen. I mean they dominated for years for free…now they can make a nice side hustle? And that’s when we realize how crazy it was to imagine we could carve up Galia amongst this very very small gaming community.

Who knows…maybe there already are people from such guilds already here. I guess we shall see. 

And yes, I agree with many above…things are going to get crazy.

author: XeanorONI
content:
There is no change without disruption, and probably most of you already developed muscle memory for SAGE Labs and navigated it with your subconciousness. In that sense, I was expecting quite some rustling in here.

Aside from that, I'm sorry there were quite a few issues still at large. We've managed to fix quite a lot of them already now but the main ones are players with many fleets experiences some kind of extreme performance degradation as well as Mobile not working as intended. I've actually spent a lot of hours optimizing the map and the menus to be mobile compatible so that's quite the letdown. I'm not sure we can fix that in the timeframe of a few hours so the decision to still host a v1 was an easy one to make. Hoping to squash the performance bug soon especially.

author: [AEP]Rhododendron
content: How many total crypto blockchain  (Solana, ATLAS, et cetera) transactions are required to craft a  "Council RFR"?  I can see on the marketplace that one RFR=4 ATLAS.  1 Atlas = approximately 0.0065 USDC as of 231222T1206z, so 4 ATLAS = approx 0.026 USDC.  In my country filing for taxes, on income is not optional.  For taxes, I am not going to manually calculate 100,000 transactions to make 10.0 USDC, and tax software charges by the number of transactions that they need to analyse, and can be 1000 USDC to analyse 10,000 transactions for example.  If it takes 100,000 Solana transactions to craft enough RFR to make 1,000 USDC, and then I have to pay 10,000 USDC for my tax software  to allow me to comply with the tax laws, then this is not profitable.  If they come up with a "denomination" of RFR that takes for example 10 blockchain transactions to craft, and then this denomination of RFR  is worth 1000 USDC, (and takes whatever time to craft in-game that makes economic sense), then this allows for me to pay for the tax software's service fee, and then have a profit afterwards, instead of a loss.  Please do not say the solution is to possibly commit tax fraud by not claiming profit that you make on a public ledger blockchain because this is a game that nearly 100% you will lose eventually.  My suggestion, that actually does work, is to make a high-denomination RFR.

author: [AEP]Rhododendron
content: you have to report every transaction done on a custodion app ? which country is this ?

reply.preview: you have to report every transaction done on a custodion app ? which country is this ?

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: The point I am trying to get at, is that every transaction you do in labs is approved on your wallet, but carried out by a custodian app. That is why you have to deposit your fleets, etc etc. Is it not possible to report larger sums, provided you are inputing and outputing at those amounts ? eg, when you withdraw say $100, you just report that withdrawal as an income ?

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: btw, I am not trying to discredit what you are saying, I am just trying to better understand exactly how you are required to declare your income tax

author: [AEP]Rhododendron
content: I am not a tax professional.  I am not giving advice.  I report all of my income, as income, to my country's "tax collector".  If someone else makes the decision to make 10000 USDC in profit, on a public blockchain, and then not report it as income, and pretend that they eventually will not be caught, and have to pay late fees and penalty fees, at a high rate of compounded interest, yearly, for several years until they are caught,  then ... that is their decision.   What I am providing feedback on, is that in my opinion S.A. should recognize that this has an impact, and that they can take it into consideration in their economic model, the amount of blockchain transactions required to make, what amount of USDC.

If it takes 100,000 Solana transactions to craft enough RFR to make 1,000 USDC, and then I have to pay 10,000 USDC for my tax software to allow me to comply with the tax laws, then this is not profitable. If they come up with a "denomination" of RFR that takes for example 10 blockchain transactions to craft, and then this denomination of RFR is worth 1000 USDC, (and takes whatever time to craft in-game that makes economic sense), then this allows for me to pay for the tax software's service fee, and then have a profit afterwards, instead of a loss. Please do not say the solution is to possibly commit tax fraud by not claiming profit that you make on a public ledger blockchain because this is a game that nearly 100% you will lose eventually. My suggestion, that actually does work, is to make a high-denomination RFR.

author: bunthius [STAR]
content: I think it's a really solid prototype. But a lot of the anims, blends and timing are roughed in just to get it playable. When our anim department does a full pass on it, I think it's gonna really shine and in the process the feel will change substantially. That's primarily the main reason it's still behind experimental toggle because it doesn't represent the full weight of all the departments yet. But it's fun already despite the raw, unpolished state so I think there's a lot of enjoyment to be had as a glimpse to where it can be. Compare R2.1 racing to R2.1.9 and you'll get a sense of how much more refined shooting will be after everyone gets their hands on it.

content:
⁠archived-foundation-room⁠   @[-U-] Orionorbit  I argued the same point yesterday.   But in my case, I believe there should of been a different manner to transition into the Mamba 3x change. 
Overall the SA Team feels justified with the Economic Warning given on Tuesday, BUT they do feel sorry about the players (small fleet owners mostly) that didn't get a chance to get a Mamba EX and where surprised by the Mamba 3x Resource Change.
These players were collateral damage in the attempt to stop the whales from pre-purchasing resources and nullifying the sudden Mamba 3x resource change.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: This is self-regulating for the most part. Production costs won't go much higher than 2 ATLAS since this is one of the only big sinks. If the ingredients cost more than 2 ATLAS, that will lessen buying pressure, which will reduce the cost of the ingredients. This is free market balance.

reply.preview: This is self-regulating for the most part. Production costs won't go much higher than 2 ATLAS since this is one of the only big sinks. If the ingredients cost more than 2 ATLAS, that will lessen buying pressure, which will reduce the cost of the ingredients. This is free market balance.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content: Yes, but I'm thinking of the imperfect market in which self-regulation lags in longer cycles.  The theory is solid, but as a Free Market, it doesn't care about algorithms at times.  Let's track the corrections and the Market Makers.  It should be interesting.

reply.preview: Yes, but I'm thinking of the imperfect market in which self-regulation lags in longer cycles. The theory is solid, but as a Free Market, it doesn't care about algorithms at times. Let's track the corrections and the Market Makers. It should be interesting.

author: ROME | Ghaz OutroONI
content: Yeah Puri is up big right now but please try to understand that it was created as a joke to have some laughs with the community here and was never suppose to be like this and it took about 4 sol and 150 usdc to set it up when it was only meant to be for laughs. Two people did their best tho and we got it out to 375 wallets in like 24 hours manually at that

author: [AEP] Freeman [FRNT]
content: Everything went out before trading started. Had 24 hours to do it and it started out as a joke. We got 20% to each the top 100 atlas locker, top 100 polis locker, top 100 discord and 20% is set aside in a multi sig wallet for our community devs/creators. I considered doing top 100 of the things you mentioned but I think only top 100 miner wallets were showing up for me on flipsyde tool at the time and I figured those guys probably are in one of those 3 categories anyway so I kept it simple. Sent everything out manually as well so I tried to keep it super simple and cheap. The more I sent to the more it costs because of the sol rent accounts. We also got 500k to 100 people on X before trading started

author: PG I BULK
content: I completely agree. Whale capital will flow in to the extent there is an opportunity for passive cash flows off of the labor force. And while access to hardware could pose challenges, we are exploring alternative avenues for these emerging market communities to engage in the economy. Mobile crew app for one, browser acces in Labs/SAGE, and also investigating various pixel streaming services. We also need lending/scholarship systems online. Nevertheless, we're going to keep forging forward with this path and these features, and I do believe a massive opportunity is present to engage both segments of the market.

reply.preview: I completely agree. Whale capital will flow in to the extent there is an opportunity for passive cash flows off of the labor force. And while access to hardware could pose challenges, we are exploring alternative avenues for these emerging market communities to engage in the economy. Mobile crew app for one, browser acces in Labs/SAGE, and also investigating various pixel streaming services. We also need lending/scholarship systems online. Nevertheless, we're going to keep forging forward with this path and these features, and I do believe a massive opportunity is present to engage both segments of the market.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: ive made numerous posts about this but here is the tl;dr. its not SA specific.  its industry wide.  Its mostly related to congestion on the network.  It's both RPCs and also on the developer/dapp side.  Basically devs didnt need to care about priority fees or compute limitations and they just did whatever they wanted because blocks were always free.   And that is OK because there are enough blockers to dev/creation anyways.   And no one expected to Solana to moon this fast.   Fast forward to now and a lot of dapps DONT have priority fees or are building their tx inefficiently.  So they have spent the last month frantically running around trying to fix it.  And a lot of it has been education to/from the RPC providers.  Xcode recently mentioned when they get back from break they need to refactor all of their code to add priority fees (or the ability to have prio fees) to most actions.  It's particularly important with complex crafting because you have several TX that rely on each other, and if one fails they all fail.  Lots of other little issues too but this is the biggest from my view point.  tl;dr congestion is very heavy on the network and RPCS/developers were not ready for it.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: It's not just that Labs was a "test".  It was just pretty standard practice to not worry about compute limits or blockheight.  And even if people were adding priority fees, it was a single lamport only.  So I wouldn't put this on SA devs at all.  Nothing to do with pressure to release Labs imo.  Just standard industry practice.  Yeah, re: priority fees thats why Phantom is working better then Solflare right now.  They impose pretty high priority fees.  Where Solflare leaves it up to the Dapps.  So our prior recommendations for everyone to swap to Solflare as sort of turned on its end.  re: solana surprise... it was a long year at sub 20. people were building as efficinetly as they could and not thinking about token price.  Hell I'd say most devs arent thinking about token price anyways.   I also expect see rapid improvement on all facets in the next couple of months.  Novemember/December was a whole lot of fact finding on what the problems were and once all the teams are back from vacation i would expect this is the number #1 problem to fix.

Regarding Solana, it shouldn't have surprised anyone. It's not the first time SOL has been this high - it was at over 200 back in Q4 2021.  The SA Team is moving toward ATLAS for this one reason (IMO).  There might be a time in which using ATLAS will be cheaper unless Solana gets its act together to keep fees low.

reply.preview: Thanks for the writeup. But if everyone adds priority fees, won't we be back to square one? Isn't is more of the code block that needs to be cleaned up? Or more RPC nodes?

author: Sammy | stegaBOB [SKR]
content: No.  Not everyone needs priority fees.  Some transactions are dependent on each other so they have to have to go first, e.g. priority fees are necessary.  So what happens if they fail repeatedly? The general answer is to just keep slamming.  That puts even more congestion on the network.  If we can sort out what actually requires priority and what doesn't then hopefully it will reduce congestion overall.   Bit of a self fulfilling prophecy. .    I think the other bit that will come from this is better error checking and handling.  Right now if we run into a block height error it just errors out the UI.  Hopefully in the future these types of errors will retry and catch more elegantly.
